risk_description: A user who can both create and approve change requests can bypass the dual-control process for system modifications
A user who can both implement and approve changes controls the entire change lifecycle without independent oversight
A user who can create emergency changes and perform audits can circumvent controls and hide evidence
A user who can both create and close incidents can fabricate and immediately resolve false incidents
A user who can delete incidents and perform audits can destroy evidence and falsify compliance records
A user who can create and delete problems can manipulate problem management records without accountability
A user who can create and delete CIs can manipulate the CMDB to hide unauthorized infrastructure changes
A user who can import CI data and audit the CMDB can load false data and certify its accuracy
A user who can both create and modify SLAs can set favorable terms and alter them without oversight
A user who can deploy releases and approve them can push unauthorized code to production
A user who can build and deploy releases without independent testing can introduce defects or malicious code
A user who controls both deployment and rollback can mask failed or unauthorized deployments
A user who can create and publish articles without review can distribute incorrect or misleading information
A user who can create and retire assets can register fictitious assets and immediately retire them for fraud
A user who can dispose of and delete asset records can steal assets and remove evidence
A user who manages licenses and audits compliance can hide license violations
A user who can create and approve service requests can self-approve requests bypassing controls
A user who can fulfill and approve requests controls the entire request lifecycle
A user who defines security policies and manages access can create policies that grant themselves excessive privileges
A user who can audit security and manage access can cover up unauthorized access grants
A user who creates security incidents and monitors alerts can suppress real alerts and create false ones
A user who configures the platform and manages user accounts has unrestricted system control
A user who customizes forms and manages workflows can alter business processes without oversight
A user who manages user accounts and performs audits can create accounts and audit their own actions
A user who can import and export data can exfiltrate sensitive information or inject malicious data
A user who can create vendors and process payments can create fictitious vendors and pay them
A user who manages contracts and processes payments can inflate contract values and process excess payments
A user who evaluates vendors and manages contracts can bias evaluations to favor specific vendors
A user who can both resolve and close incidents may prematurely close incidents without proper resolution
A user who schedules and implements changes can schedule changes outside maintenance windows
A user who performs root cause analysis and closes problems can close without adequate investigation
A user who can delete and close incidents can eliminate audit trail of incident handling
A user who creates CIs and manages baselines can create unauthorized CIs and include them in baselines
A user who plans and approves releases can plan and self-approve without independent review
A user who administers the KB and publishes articles can bypass knowledge review processes
A user who procures assets and audits them can order unnecessary assets and certify the inventory
A user who responds to security incidents and manages policies can alter policies to cover up breaches
A user who manages backups and configurations can alter configs and restore old states to hide changes
A user who designs workflows and approval chains can bypass approval requirements entirely
A user who can perform bulk updates and export data can manipulate large datasets and extract them
A user with API access and user admin can automate unauthorized account creation
A user who creates standard and emergency changes can classify changes as emergency to skip approvals
A user who declares major incidents and creates reports can exaggerate or downplay incident severity
A user who can delete CIs and export CMDB data can remove CIs after exporting sensitive infrastructure info
A user who manages licenses and can delete asset records can hide unlicensed software
A user who manages catalog and fulfills requests can create and fulfill self-serving catalog items
A user who manages vulnerabilities and access can exploit known vulnerabilities through access manipulation
A user who manages integrations and platform config can create unauthorized data pathways
A user who configures notifications and manages users can suppress alerts for specific accounts
A user who creates audit reports and manages policies can write policies to match their own audit findings
A user who assesses risk and monitors compliance can understate risks and certify compliance falsely
A user who creates vendors and evaluates them can establish and favorably rate new vendors
A user who can approve and reject changes has unchecked authority over the change process
A user who creates known errors and closes problems can prematurely mark problems as known without proper fix
A user who reviews and retires articles can remove valid knowledge without peer oversight
A user who updates and disposes assets can change asset details before disposal to obscure theft
A user who monitors security and generates reports can selectively report security events
A user who creates reports and exports data can design reports to extract sensitive information
A user who escalates incidents and declares major incidents can unnecessarily elevate minor issues
A user who reviews and closes changes controls the post-implementation assessment without independent check
